merger_name: DP World Global Forwarding – Pure Global Logistics

acquirers.name: DP WORLD GLOBAL FORWARDING PTY LTD

other_parties.name: DP WORLD LOGISTICS OCEANIA (HOLDING) PTY LTD

merger_description:
DPW Global Forwarding Pty Ltd (t/as **DP World Global Forwarding**) proposes to acquire 100% of the shares in Pac Ware Pty Ltd (t/as **Pure Global Logistics**), under a Share Sale Agreement (the **Acquisition**).

DP World Global Forwarding provides contract logistics and freight forwarding services in Australia, which includes warehousing, fulfilment and e-commerce support.

DP World Global Forwarding’s services encompass freight forwarding by ocean, air, road and rail transportation across Australia, including associated warehousing and logistics support. DP World Global Forwarding is a 100% owned subsidiary of DP World Logistics Oceania (Holding) Pty Ltd, which is ultimately controlled by DP World Limited, a United Arab Emirates entity.

DP World Australia Limited (**DP World Australia**) is a connected entity of DP World Global Forwarding and operates container terminals and provides container stevedoring services at each of the Ports of Melbourne, Botany (Sydney), Brisbane and Fremantle.

Pure Global Logistics is an Australian freight forwarding business specialising in air and sea transportation and customs clearance, primarily for fashion and discount retailers in Australia.

Both DP World Global Forwarding and Pure Global Logistics supply international freight forwarding services to Australian customers (mostly to customers importing, rather than exporting, goods).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Philip Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
DPW Global Forwarding Pty Ltd (DP World Global Forwarding)
applied for a notification waiver in respect of its proposed
acquisition of 100% of the share capital in Pac Ware Pty Ltd (Pure
Global Logistics), as described in the transaction documents
provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.

The acquirer, DP World Global Forwarding, provides contract
logistics and freight forwarding services in Australia, which includes
warehousing, fulfilment and e-commerce support.
DPW Global Forwarding’s services encompass freight forwarding by
ocean, air, road and rail transportation across Australia, including
associated warehousing and logistics support. DPW Global
Forwarding is a 100% owned subsidiary of DP World Logistics
Oceania (Holding) Pty Ltd, which is ultimately controlled by DP World
Limited, a United Arab Emirates entity.
DP World Australia Limited is a connected entity of DP World Global
Forwarding and operates container terminals and provides container
stevedoring services at each of the Ports of Melbourne, Botany
(Sydney), Brisbane and Fremantle.
The target, Pure Global Logistics, is an Australian freight forwarding
business specialising in air and sea transportation and customs
clearance, primarily for fashion and discount retailers in Australia.
DP World Global Forwarding and Pure Global Logistics are each
active in the supply of supply international freight forwarding
services to customers in Australia.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. there is a small combined share between DP World Global
Forwarding and Pure Global Logistics in the supply of freight
forwarding services in Australia
b. there are alternative suppliers of freight forwarding services
to customers in Australia
c. there is a low risk of foreclosure or other concerning vertical
or conglomerate effects resulting from exclusionary
conduct, bundling or tying of services in the logistics supply
chain in Australia post-acquisition.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.
